sentences.text: All right.
We're going to read the slate again because of an accidental misspelling.
The date is March 21, 1996.
The place, Washington, DC, in the United States.
The subject of the interview, Mr.
Walter Rockler.
The interviewer, Bruce Einhorn.
The language of the interview, English.
Well, good morning.
Please state and spell your name, if you would.
Walter Rockler, R-O-C-K-L-E-R.
In the Washington suburbs, Bethesda, Maryland.
Minneapolis, Minnesota.
November 25, 1920, which was a memorable day because it was also Thanksgiving.
Nathan.
No, no, no.
It's the original name, and I think it's Rumanian-derived.
I have a pretty good idea.
I think he was born in a town-- relatively small town-- on the Russian-Rumanian border called 'Falashti,' F-A-L-A-S-H-T-I.
No, because he doesn't remember-- he did not remember.
I don't even think he had a birth certificate.
He was-- it was probably 1898 or 1899.
All his life he was a furrier.
I've been trying to recollect that.
I think around 1970.
In Chicago, Illinois.
Lung cancer.
Depends on what spelling you prefer.
The original Russian name was-- her first name was Evelyn [RUSSIAN].
Don't ask me to spell it.
It was shortened when she came to the United States to [?
Terfanski. ?]
She was born, I think in, Belz, then-czarist Russia.
It's the regional capital for the area, which is Moldavia.
It's Kishinev, and it was some kilometers away from Kishinev.
It itself is a fair-sized town.
Oh, yes.
When she first came to the United States, she worked in department stores in Minneapolis and Chicago.
And then for a long period of time when she had children, she stayed at home, and then she worked with my father in his business.
She's still alive.
She's in Los Angeles, California.
No.
They were both in the United States before World War I.
Yes.
A younger-- younger sister and brother.
I sure do, 1925 and 1929.
No, I'm the eldest, my sister is next, and my brother is the youngest.
Iris.
Schoenfeld is her married name.
Allan.
Yes, they're both alive.
Los Angeles.
No.
Three years.
Chicago.
First, through my college years, which would mean up to about the age of 19, and then more recently, between 1954 and 1966, I practiced law there.
A semi-slum area on the west side, which was basically the Jewish community there.
But at one time or another I've lived in almost every part of Chicago, including the North Shore suburbs, which are relatively affluent.
Jewish, but not intensely religious at all.
No.
I-- I've never had any synagogue that I visited a lot.
But I did have a bar mitzvah when I was young, and if the affiliation was anything, it was orthodox.
Yes.
Public school, Von Steuben.
Von Steuben.
High school? 1937.
No.
It was really very simple.
I just went to classes regularly and scored.
That was what it was all about.
Winning.
Winning.
No, I have-- I hadn't any well-defined talents, and I had no well-defined ambitions.
Yes.
University of Chicago.
I concentrated on whatever [LAUGHS] the studies were.
No, I had a scholarship there, and for the first two years of the University of Chicago, you had a-- practically a fixed curriculum.
This was in the days of Robert Maynard Hutchins as president and chancellor, and it was diversified.

But-- but-- but my area of specialization was so-called 'social studies department,' and my emphasis was heavily on political science and economics-- what is called 'political science.' It's political, all right, but not so science.
I walked from home.
We-- I lived within about a mile and a half, and I walled back and forth every day.
It was chilly, very chilly.
It was my career.
I mean, my specialty when I was young was going to school.
And that's what I did.
I generally took twice the required number of courses, so I finished in three years.
Yes.
I was the first member of my extended family to attend the university, I mean, among all my cousins, who were numerous.
Well, my grandmother lived with us, so-- and she never bothered to learn English.
She lived in the United States for 40 years.
She understood some, but she never spoke English.
Maternal grandmother.
She didn't favor any.
She just knew one, which was Yiddish.
Oh, it was the first language I learned.
Of course, I learned English almost simultaneously.
Not a lot.
My parents generally did not speak Yiddish, although they could when they felt like it.
They did not speak Yiddish with a view to concealing anything from the kids because we knew Yiddish.
Oh, yes.
My mother spoke almost flawless English, no Kissinger-type accent or any other kind of accent.
She made it a point to learn to speak very unaccented English.
My father had an accent.
When I was young, I didn't know it.
It seemed perfectly natural English to me.
Yeah, my father became a citizen by virtue of being in the American Army in World War I.
Missouri, as a mule driver.
Huh?
No, uh-uh.
He had just arrived in the States.
He would-- he'd been in the States for about a year or two and didn't really speak English very well when he was in the army.
My mother got her citizenship derivatively, by marriage.
No.
No.
Well, I was, but kind of marginally in college.
In the-- in the late '30s, there was a big student organization, national in character, called the American Student Union.
Around 1939, it split.
And it really split because there was some general feeling that the communists had taken it over at that time.
This was around the time, I think-- I'm not sure of the exact dates, but I think 1940 was the year that the Soviet Union invaded Finland, which seemed to produce a lot of agitation.

And I went with the split-off group, which was anti-communist, which-- which got-- I can't remember exactly the name of it, but I think it became National Student something to distinguish itself from the ASU.
I was educated at the University of Chicago.
Obviously, I was a right-winger in those days.
The University of Chicago never produced anything but right-wingers.
Yeah, my family, like all immigrant families, would-- voted Democratic.
But I had great doubts that the Democrats were on the path to virtue.
At the University of Chicago, 1939.
Oh yeah, going back and forth to classes, listening to lectures, like Edward Benes, who was at the University of Chicago in '39.
Well, Professor Benes had been prime minister or president-- I forget what the title was-- of Czechoslovakia when the Germans marched which was 1939.
The Germans moved into Czechoslovakia in March 1939.
They quickly made it evident that his presence was not desired in Europe, and if he stayed very long, he might not even be in Europe or anywhere else.
No, he was a lecturer.
I don't know that he did any classes, but he-- he turned up at the University of Chicago, which had all kinds of interesting people.
Somewhere along the-- that time, we had Bertrand Russell there too.
Sure.
I also attended Russell's lectures.
I attended everything that was around.
Extremely familiar.
This was an area in which I was interested and also was part of my curricular studies.
For example, somewhere around the age of 16, I read from cover to cover.
And I'm probably one of the few people alive today who ever did read it.
Yeah.
I had a pretty good idea of what the Nazis were up to.
Not while I was at the University-- not in-- not up to 1940, no.
Although by that time, we had refugees turning up as students-- German Jews, primarily, who were-- had been extremely well connected in Germany.
You know, these were people who had titles, who were associated with the Kaiser's court, who were well-to-do, who were more German than they were either American or Jewish.
Any group that I was connected with was anti-Nazi.
There was no question of that.
It was-- it was after college, at the law school, that I became more active, although not tremendously active.
While I was at law school, there were a bunch of Anglophiles who had an interest in supporting the British against the Germans in 1940 and '41, and I became a part of that group.
The notable figure there was Bill Bundy, who was a classmate of mine, who later became a director of the CIA.
Pardon me?
Harvard.
In the law library at Harvard Law School.
Surprise.
Not altogether surprise, and I'll tell you why.
But I was sitting up in the-- there was a typewriting enclave where you could type your notes.
And I noticed a big stir in the middle of the library, which was unusual.
This was Sunday afternoon, December 7, obviously.
And a bunch of students were congregated around the central desk of the library, so I finally decided I'd quit typing notes and go down and find out what was going on.
And somebody said the Japanese had bombed Pearl Harbor.
Well, it was a surprise.
The concrete event was a surprise.
But I had started studying Japanese extra-curricularly.
I was in law school in the Harvard College in September of that year, which was about three months before the war, which was probably a little foresighted.
I took courses there with a fellow named Reischauer, who later became ambassador to Japan.
Oh, I should say it affected my education.
It affected my outlook, too, beyond my education.
As soon as-- as soon as the war was declared, the question was what part of the military was I going to go into?
And of course, the law school took the same view.
They-- the second year in law school was almost obliterated by the start of the war.
I think they did not run regular examinations.
If you left somewhere after the midpoint of the year, you got credit for the year even though you learned very, very little in those days.
And that happened to me.
Within, I think, a month of the outbreak of war.
I was signed up for the Navy.
I was I don't think at that time even registered for the draft, but I was a volunteer.
No, because otherwise I wouldn't have started Japanese three months before that time.
I was fairly aware of the fact that the Japanese were militaristic and expansion-minded, and the United States was cramping their style.
The war was an outbreak, in part, of American demands on Japan, with threats that we'd cut off their oil supplies and other-- other supplies.
And I don't think anybody except people who were really much more knowledgeable than I was expected the war to break out then.
There were some indications that relations between Japan and the United States were not very good at that time.
And of course, historically, the Japanese had tied themselves up in the so-called 'axis' with Germany and Italy, the so-called anti-democratic forces.
Yeah, I did.
I entered in April, but I think I was signed up by January or February.
Because the Navy was running a Japanese language school, and that's what I entered.
Boulder, Colorado, attached to the University of Colorado.
It's hard to say what it was like.
It was six hours a day of Japanese classes, three, four hours of studying Japanese, strenuous efforts and very little production.
And this was true not only for me but for most of the class.
There were a handful of people in the class who had a real affinity for studying languages who very quickly developed either some spoken or written skills.
And in fact, in the post-war period, all of the key translators of Japanese literature, classic and modern, were classmates of mine in the Navy Japanese Language School.
But in retrospect, I went to a reunion of the Japanese Language School of the Navy, and I have a son who lives in Boulder who said, what kind of crazy project was that?
Why would you ever go study Japanese when the United States had literally tens of thousands of Nisei with Japanese background who could speak Japanese?
I had to explain to him at great length that by virtue of the paranoia in the United States and the racism that was rampant, the Nisei were out of the question for the Navy.
The Army took Nisei in as translators.
I ultimately wound up, as I will tell you, in the Marine Corps.
And the Marine Corps would borrow Army teams, but they would not use Nisei as translators directly in the Marine Corps.
Yeah, [LAUGHS] I was a yeoman second class in civilian clothes.
All during the time I was in the Navy, I never put on a-- a sailor suit.
No, this was Boulder, Colorado.
There were very few ships there.
When I went in the Marine Corps.
About the early part or middle of 1943.
They asked-- they asked for volunteers, and since I was not a very bright kid at that time, I volunteered for the Marine Corps.
I must-- I must have had some form of death wish.
Initially, we were sent out-- there must have been about 40 or 50 of us altogether.
The class consisted of maybe 200, so roughly a quarter of the class went into the Marine Corps.
It was volunteers.
Of the Boulder class, yeah.
Boulder ultimately must have turned out about 1,000 people, but I was in almost the first class, and it was roughly 200 people.
I think the Marine group was sent to a Camp Elliott near San Diego for a period of two, three months, in which we got our limited military training.
In other words, they showed us what a rifle looked like and a few other important things like that.
We learned to march.
Oh yeah.
By the time I was in the Marine Corps, I had to go buy uniforms.
And they were very stylish uniforms.
I liked them.
Never wore them much.
Up till the end of that year.
Now, let's see.
That's right, up-- up to about December.

But then my group-- by my group-- I said there were 40 or 50 people who went into the Marine Corps. 10 or 12-- I think 12-- were then earmarked for one of the new Marine divisions that were being formed, in this case, the 4th Marine Division.
And the complement for the 4th Marine Division, which was just being put together, consisted of a bunch of odd units here and there in the Marine Corps being reformulated in a new organization.

The-- the division itself was just being put together, and to put us in some kind of standby status, we were then shipped, I think 12 of us, to an Army camp in Japanese language located near Minneapolis-- Camp Savage, Minnesota.
Attended more Japanese classes.
It was an interesting camp, because it had Army people.
It also had Canadian army people.
And then it had 12 Marines.
And as far as I know, we were the only Marines in the city of Minneapolis.
I was married, so that put me under certain handicaps, but the rest of my colleagues had a damn good times.
When was I married?
June of 1942.
No, it was even before that.
In Chicago.
Elsie Spira, S-P-I-R-A.
Oh yeah.
She went through school with me in Chicago.
Yes.
Oh, I think two, three months.
We were put on ice until the end of the year, when we joined the division in California.
The 4th Division was formed sometime in the fall of '43.
Camp Pendleton, I think.
Oh yes.
It's near Oceanside, California, between San Diego and Los Angeles.
She lived in La Jolla.
I mean, she had an apartment there.
But I would see her on weekends.
They had no options.
I did it myself.
I mean, they were presented with a bunch of facts.
I didn't consult them.
Bear in mind, all of this was done when I was living in-- my family was in Chicago.
I was in Boston or Cambridge, Massachusetts, going to law school.
No, there was nothing to talk about.
They didn't have any reaction.
I think they kind of expected I would do something like that.
They knew I-- they knew I had peculiarities.
He was much too young.
He was maybe 10 years old at this time.
The service wasn't desperate enough to look at him.
Huh?
In a divorce.
No, no.
I was with the 4th Marine Division from its inception until it was disbanded in 19-- the end of '45 or '46.
So I went wherever the division went.
Well, the first thing we did was around the end of '43-- get on board ship to go Kwajalein Atoll, where we were scheduled to take a couple of Japanese-held islands, which we did.
I remember I was assigned some duty function on New Year's Eve 1943, '44, which was kind of grim because we were getting aboard ship the next day or two.
And then somewhere along the line, I had a couple days off, so I went up to Los Angeles.
At that time, nobody in my family lived in Los Angeles.

It was just a-- an unknown city, for all practical purposes. remember talking to the French mistress of ceremonies at some bar or a hotel who assured me-- and I liked this very well-- that I would survive the war.
About 2,000 miles west of Hawaii.
We were on board ship for more than a month.
We stopped in Hawaii and then went further on into this atoll.
The atoll was taken by the 4th Marine Division, I think the 7th Army Division.
The 7th Army Division took Kwajalein Island, which was the biggest island in the group, and the Marine division took a couple of subsidiary islands called Roi and Namur.
But it was a huge atoll, you know, the-- the-- which was really a-- a circular coral reef surrounded a-- a lagoon.
We took this-- I think it must have been January, February 1944.
Then the division left the atoll, but I was left behind with a-- with a-- another lieutenant and I think four or five enlisted men on a couple of the outlying islands of Kwajalein Atoll.
And I was there when it was bombed by the Japanese a couple weeks after we took it.
That's [LAUGHS] a very good question.
I didn't ask anybody.
They just left me.
Well, I was the occupation force.
All five or six of us were the occupation force.
The inhabitants consisted of maybe 300 Micronesian natives.
Micronesian not being Melanesian-- they're-- they're different.
As a matter of fact, the natives identified me as a missionary who'd been there about 15 years before, and I became Lockwood of the islands.
They just thought that all whites looked alike.
And they were great singers.
I mean, we would sit out on the island, and they would sing in six-part harmony.
I mean, they were incredibly good singers.
No.
I am not only not an incredibly good singer, I'm a terrible singer.
It was very interesting because there-- there was another lieutenant who came from Indiana, and we had cornered the regimental supply of canned roast beef and pineapple.
And we lived on pineapple and roast beef all the time, which was pretty good.
We had no duties whatever.
